Gerakan Indonesia Mengajar
Founder
2010 - Present (16 years)
Jakarta, Indonesia

In 2010, I initiated and established the Gerakan Indonesia Mengajar alongside

a group of colleagues. This program sends graduates to teach at elementary

schools in remote areas of Indonesia, attracting university graduates to roles

they might not typically consider.

The movement's appeal stems from a narrative that presents teaching in

remote areas as a noble pursuit. Over 200,000 graduates have applied to

become Young Educators, undergoing a selection process for the opportunity

to serve in rural areas.

The program has three main objectives: driving sustainable educational

change in remote villages, cultivating future leaders with global competencies

and grassroots understanding, and fostering a social education movement

across Indonesia.

From its inception, the movement has operated independently of government

funding, relying on volunteer support and corporate social responsibility

contributions. This principle of independence was maintained even during my

tenure as Minister of Education and Culture.

Since 2010, the program has received applications from 204,607 Indonesian

graduates, with 1,193 Young Educators selected to serve in 349 remote

villages. The program continues to attract a high number of applicants.

The success of Gerakan Indonesia Mengajar is a testament to the collective

efforts of all involved, from the Young Educators to the supporting staff and

partner organizations. It demonstrates the potential of collaborative initiatives

in reshaping perceptions about service and leadership among Indonesia's

youth, building upon the legacy of the nation's founding fathers.

Pemerintah Provinsi DKI Jakarta
Governor of Jakarta
October 2017 - October 2022 (5 years 1 month)

Jakarta, Indonesia

During my tenure as Governor of Jakarta from October 2017 to October 2022,

I oversaw the administration of one of the world's largest metropolises, with

a population of eleven million. This role involved managing 180,000 civil

servants and non-governmental workers, and an annual budget exceeding IDR

80 trillion.

A significant focus was placed on transforming Jakarta's public transportation

sector. Over three years, the administration integrated all public transportation,

doubled the coverage area, and tripled daily ridership. As a result, Jakarta

became the first Southeast Asian city to receive the Sustainable Transport

Award in 2021.

During the pandemic, the administration implemented science-based

policies prioritizing safety and transparency. These decisions were grounded

in scientific knowledge and evidence, sometimes diverging from central

government or public opinion.

The administration worked on providing equal development opportunities for

urban neighborhoods and enhancing the capacity of small and micro-scale

entrepreneurs. Efforts were also made in various areas including public health,

inclusivity, and city planning initiatives.

Notable projects included the completion of the Jakarta International

Stadium and the implementation of integrated public transportation. Systemic

innovations were also introduced, such as the Smart Planning system,

achieving Unqualified Opinion accounting status for five consecutive years,

reforming the student admission system, and prioritizing policies for vulnerable

groups.

The administration's efforts received recognition, including the ASEAN

ICT Awards Gold Medal and the Sustainable Transport Award. These

achievements were the result of collaborative efforts between the Jakarta

administration team and the city's residents, whose contributions were

instrumental in implementing these initiatives and driving positive change in

Jakarta.

From 2021 to 2022, during my term as Governor of Jakarta, I served as Vice

Chairman of the C40 Advisory Board. The C40 is a network of mayors from

nearly one hundred leading cities worldwide, focused on addressing climate

change and promoting sustainable urban development.

The C40 network facilitates collaboration among city leaders, enabling the

exchange of ideas and practices for creating greener, healthier, and more

prosperous cities. It addresses areas including clean energy, sustainable

transportation, waste management, and climate resilience.

I was elected as one of the Vice Chairmen of the Advisory Board, alongside

Governor Keiko of Tokyo, under the chairmanship of Sadiq Khan, the Mayor of

London. This marked the first time an Indonesian figure held a top leadership

position within the C40 network.

This role provided an opportunity to contribute to global discussions on urban

sustainability and to share Jakarta's experiences on an international platform.

It also allowed for the exchange of insights and strategies with other world-

class cities for potential implementation in Jakarta.

Being part of the C40 leadership emphasized the role of cities in driving

global change and the importance of collaborative efforts in addressing urban

challenges.

This position offered a chance to represent both Jakarta and Indonesia on a

global stage. The experience provided insights into international approaches to

creating sustainable, resilient, and inclusive urban environments.

Minister of Education and Culture

Jakarta, Indonesia

During my tenure as Minister of Education and Culture from October 2014

to July 2016, several initiatives were implemented to improve education and

culture in Indonesia.

The ministry introduced the Education Balance Sheet, providing transparent

performance reports on education for provinces, districts, and cities across

Indonesia. This tool enabled stakeholders to track fund allocation, teacher

quality, school conditions, and student performance more effectively.

Page 6 of 10

 
 
 
Significant changes were made to the national exam system. The exam

was repurposed as a tool for educational mapping rather than a graduation

requirement. The ministry also introduced computer-based national exams,

which were rapidly adopted across educational institutions.

To address the need for educators in remote and challenging areas, the

Frontline Teachers program was launched. The ministry also led Indonesia's

participation in the Frankfurt Book Fair, resulting in an increase in the

translation of Indonesian literary works.

For cultural heritage preservation, an online registration system for cultural

heritage items was implemented, exceeding initial targets. The Learning with

Maestros program was another initiative that allowed students to engage with

the creative processes of arts and culture maestros.

These efforts were part of a broader strategy to enhance education and

cultural preservation in Indonesia. The success of these initiatives was largely

due to the hard work and dedication of the ministry staff and educational

professionals across the country. Their commitment and expertise were

instrumental in implementing these programs and driving positive change in

Indonesia's education and cultural sectors.

Kelas Inspirasi
Founder
2012 - 2014 (2 years)
Jakarta, Indonesia

In 2012, we initiated the Gerakan Kelas Inspirasi after observing that most

schools focused primarily on teaching, with few emphasizing education, and

almost none prioritizing inspiration. This movement invites accomplished

professionals to return to schools as one-day teachers, sharing their expertise

and its societal impact.

The response from professionals was overwhelming, with the initiative quickly

spreading from Jakarta across Indonesia. The movement grew organically

through self-funding and volunteer efforts in various cities and districts. I often

remarked that goodness is contagious, with volunteers inspiring one another

through their kindness.

Page 7 of 10

 
 
 
Participating professionals find joy in reconnecting with schools and mentoring

younger generations. For students, these professionals serve as inspirational

figures, igniting aspirations. The emotional connections formed benefit all

involved, fostering a sense of national contribution.

By 2023, Gerakan Kelas Inspirasi had reached 1,449 schools across 182 cities

and districts in Indonesia. It's coordinated by 2,452 volunteer organizers, with

thousands of professionals taking time off work to teach. This commitment

demonstrates their dedication to contributing meaningfully.

The movement's organic growth has involved diverse segments of society,

creating new partnerships between schools and educational supporters

nationwide. Through Gerakan Kelas Inspirasi, we've aimed to showcase how

ideas and narratives can inspire and positively impact both direct participants

and the broader community.

Universitas Paramadina
Rector
2007 - 2014 (7 years)
Jakarta, Indonesia

As Rector of Universitas Paramadina from 2007 to 2014, I led a dedicated

team in transforming the university's academic landscape. Our efforts focused

on implementing innovative programs to enhance the educational experience

and foster leadership among students.

One of our key initiatives was the Dual Transcript Program, which encouraged

active student engagement in campus activities. This program resulted in

graduates receiving both academic and non-academic transcripts, providing

a comprehensive record of their university experience. We also introduced an

Anti-Corruption Course as a core module, making Universitas Paramadina the

first institution to mandate such a course.

Our commitment to integrity extended beyond our campus. We coordinated

the Indonesia Integrity Education Network, uniting over 140 universities in the

country to promote anti-corruption education. To support talented students

from diverse backgrounds, we established the Paramadina Fellowship and

Social Responsibility programs, offering full scholarships to high-achieving and

economically disadvantaged students.

management, international relations, and communication, with strong support

from the business community. Our efforts to implement modern management

practices garnered trust from various stakeholders, leading to increased

financial support and campus expansion.

The university also became a hub for thought leadership, establishing several

think tanks and actively participating in discussions on governance, economy,

society, and culture. We fostered international collaborations, including

exchange programs with Australia and New Zealand, to enrich our faculty's

knowledge and cultural experiences.

Throughout my time as Rector, our focus remained on cultivating leadership,

entrepreneurship, and ethics among our students, preparing them to make

meaningful contributions to society.

Gerakan Indonesia Menyala
Founder
2012 - 2013 (1 year)
Jakarta, Indonesia

In 2012, we initiated the Gerakan Indonesia Menyala after witnessing the lack

of reading materials in remote villages during my visits. This movement aimed

to establish libraries in these areas, responding to the aspirations of Young

Educators serving there.

We called upon urban communities to donate books, and the response

was overwhelming. Within a short period, the movement spread to twenty

cities, with 895 recorded volunteers and many more unaccounted for. These

volunteers, often established professionals, were driven by a genuine desire to

make a difference.

Over the course of a year, we collected more than 150,000 books. These

were distributed to 115 remote villages, establishing reading corners and

contributing to the creation of 36 reading gardens across Greater Jakarta. The

impact was significant, providing these communities with their own libraries

and reading spaces.

The movement's success was amplified by the voluntary contributions of

logistics and cargo companies, who waived shipping costs despite the

Page 9 of 10

 
 
 
substantial weight of the book packages. This demonstrated how the right

ideas and narrative could mobilize various parties to participate actively.

We named it Gerakan Indonesia Menyala (Indonesia Illuminated Movement)

to symbolize how the knowledge in these books would guide readers from

darkness to light. The establishment of libraries in remote villages was

envisioned as a way to illuminate the nation, bringing enlightenment to its

people.

This initiative exemplifies how a well-conceived idea, combined with

community engagement, can create substantial positive change. It showcases

the power of collective action in addressing educational disparities and

promoting literacy across Indonesia.
